coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis and performance of pH/temperature dual-response hydrogel containing lignin-based carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Lan
Mo, Zhenye
Li, Qiong
Zheng, Dafeng
Qiu, Xueqing
Pan, Xuejun
coredata.dc:description: This study demonstrated a facile method to synthesize lignin-based carbon dots (L-CDs) first. Results indicated that the L-CDs had a diameter of 2–5 nm and a graphene-like crystalline structure. It was found that under the optimal synthesis conditions, the fluorescence lifetime of L-CDs was about 12 ns. Within the range of pH 1–10, the fluorescence intensity of the L-CDs and pH value followed a linear relationship. With the contribution of L-CDs, pH/temperature dual responsive hydrogel was synthesized. The elastic modulus G′ of hydrogel was much higher than viscous modulus G″. When the PVA content was larger than 10 wt%, the temperature sensitivity and water retention rate gradually decreased. The skeleton of hydrogels had a typical porous honeycomb structure, which made it possible to control its internal pore size by adjusting the content of PVA. There was a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of hydrogels and pH value in the range of pH 1–7. Therefore, the pH/temperature dual responsive hydrogel presented a new route for designing tissue engineering scaffolds and drug carriers.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis and performance of pH/temperature dual-response hydrogel containing lignin-based carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study demonstrated a facile method to synthesize lignin-based carbon dots (L-CDs) first. Results indicated that the L-CDs had a diameter of 2–5 nm and a graphene-like crystalline structure. It was found that under the optimal synthesis conditions, the fluorescence lifetime of L-CDs was about 12 ns. Within the range of pH 1–10, the fluorescence intensity of the L-CDs and pH value followed a linear relationship. With the contribution of L-CDs, pH/temperature dual responsive hydrogel was synthesized. The elastic modulus G′ of hydrogel was much higher than viscous modulus G″. When the PVA content was larger than 10 wt%, the temperature sensitivity and water retention rate gradually decreased. The skeleton of hydrogels had a typical porous honeycomb structure, which made it possible to control its internal pore size by adjusting the content of PVA. There was a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of hydrogels and pH value in the range of pH 1–7. Therefore, the pH/temperature dual responsive hydrogel presented a new route for designing tissue engineering scaffolds and drug carriers.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignin is the second abundant and renewable biomass resource on the earth [1]. Although plants can produce up to 150 billion tons of lignin on earth every year through photosynthesis, only less than 5% was used in different applications, such as concrete water reducer [2], cement grinding aid [3], pesticide dispersant [4] and dye dispersant [5]. In order to make sustainable use of lignin further, researchers have utilized lignin to product valuable carbon materials, such as activated carbons [6], templated carbons [7], mesoporous carbon monoliths [8], carbon nanofibers [9] and carbon dots [10]. Carbon quantum dots, as one of the youngest members in the carbon material family [11], have attracted increasing attention of researchers since it was initially found in 2004 [12]. Carbon dots have excellent water dispersibility, biocompatibility, light stability, non-flickering photoluminescence, and adjustable excitation and emission wavelengths, which are the advantages that the traditional fluorescent organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots do not have. Therefore, carbon dots have great potential to be used in biological imaging [13], drug delivery [14,15], sensors [16], photocatalysis [17], and nanoelectronic devices [18].

Generally, carbon dots are fluorescent nano‑carbon materials with a particles size smaller than 10 nm [19]. The preparation strategies of carbon dots reported in literature can be simply classified into two types. One is “top-down” strategy, and the other is “bottom-up” strategy. Arc discharge [20], laser ablation [21], plasma synthesis [22], template [23] and ultrasonic methods [24] belong to the “top-down” strategy. Electrochemical [25], microwave, ultrasonic synthesis [26] and hydrothermal methods fall into the “bottom-up” strategy. Among these methods, the hydrothermal synthesis is a simple method without special equipment requirements and thereby a promising and competitive one for the synthesis of carbon dots.

In the past ten years, various substances have been used as the precursors of carbon dots, such as o-phenylenediamine [27], rhodamine [28], and thiourea [29]. With the advantages of non-toxicity, renewability and good biocompatibility, natural materials have been used in more and more studies as precursor of carbon dots, such as spider silk [30], apple juice [31], hair [32], cellulose [33], and even solid wastes including grass [34], waste onion skin [35], and paper ash [36].

With high carbon content, aromatic conjugate structure, endogenous heteroatom doping and chemical modification, lignin is an ideal carbon source for carbon dots. Shi et al. [37] prepared lignin-based carbon dots with the fluorescent quantum yield of 8.1% using alkali lignin, ethylenediamine and formaldehyde as the raw materials in a nitrogen atmosphere at 300 °C. Jiang et al. [38] prepared lignin carbon dots with a fluorescent quantum yield of 7.95% from lignin, betaine, and lactic acid when heated to 300 °C. Li et al. [36] prepared carbon dots from purified lignin with a yield of 14.24% through hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C. Chen et al. [39] prepared carbon dots with the particle size of 2–10 nm from lignin and H2O2. It is apparent that the fluorescent quantum yield of reported lignin-based carbon dots was rather low, and functionalization and valorization of the lignin-based carbon dots need further investigation.

Hydrogel is a water-soluble polymer with the network cross-linked structure. Its hydrophilic groups are bound to water molecules through and the water molecules are wrapped in the network, while hydrophobic groups expand with water. Hydrogels have been widely applied in biomedical engineering fields such as tissue engineering scaffolds, drug carriers, and so on. The research of fluorescence hydrogels related to carbon quantum dots mainly focused on the specific response of metal ions and other fields. Cheng et al. [40] added PEI-CD into the mixture of microcrystalline cellulose, sodium hydroxide and urea. After stirring for 1 h, the fluorescent intelligent hydrogels can be prepared by dropping ethanol. In the range of Fe3+ from 10 μmol/L to 100 μmol/L, the relative fluorescence intensity of the hydrogels showed a good linear relationship with the Fe3+ concentration in the solution, and its fluorescence color gradually changed from bright blue to light blue with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. Using lanthanide ions, silk fibroin derived carbon dots and Pectin/PVA hydrogel (PPH), Karen [41] synthesized a tough LPPH hydrogels, which can response to both pH and metal ions. The PL intensity ratios of the hydrogel at 473 nm and 617 nm showed a significant change at different pH values, indicating that pH response can be quantified in high sensitivity. In addition, they had different chromic responses to metal ions like Fe2+ and Fe3 + .

In this study, we developed a new and simple process for the synthesis of lignin-based carbon dots with high fluorescent quantum yield and pH-responsibility first. Lignin, citric acid, and ethylenediamine were used as raw materials. The synthesis process was optimized to maximize the fluorescence lifetime. The structure and the pH-responsibility of the lignin-based carbon dots were investigated. The present study provided a new high-value application for the lignin-based carbon dots. Besides, we established a simple synthesis method for the pH/temperature dual responsive hydrogel. L-CDs, NIPAM and PVA were used as the main raw materials, the fluorescent hydrogels with pH/temperature double response were rapidly synthesized by free radical polymerization. The effects of PVA usage on the functional groups, temperature responsiveness, rheological properties and micromorphology of hydrogels were studied. Then, the pH response of the composite hydrogel was constructed by using the prepared lignin-based carbon dots as the pH fluorescence probe, and the fluorescence intensity of the composite hydrogel under different pH was studied. This synthetic method provides a new direction for the preparation of responsive multifunctional hydrogels, and also presents a new idea for widening the application scope of hydrogels.

Lignin was isolated from corncob using the p-toluenesulfonic acid method, as previously described [42]. Citric acid monohydrate (CA) was purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Ethylene Diamine (EDA) was purchased from Jiangsu Yonghua Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Suzhou, China). Potassium bromide (KBr) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were analytical grade and used as received. Deionized (DI) water was used for preparation of all solutions. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N. N ‘- methylene bisacrylamide (MBA) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N. N, n ‘, n′ - tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd.(Shanghai, China). Ammonium persulfate (APS) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

EDA can be used not only as a precursor of carbon dots, but also as a main source of nitrogen. In this study, EDA (2.40 g) was dissolved in DI water (60 g) to prepare an EDA solution. A certain mass of solid lignin (0.05 g, 0.075 g and 0.1 g) and 2.10 g citric acid monohydrate were added to the EDA solution. After being ultrasonicated for 10 min, the mixture was transferred to a high-pressure reactor, heated to a certain temperature at a heating rate of 5 °C/min and then kept at the temperature for a certain period of time. The reaction mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane to remove the unreacted lignin particles. The clear filtrate was dialyze through a 1000 Da dialysis membrane to remove ultra-small molecules and ions. After being dialyzed for three days, the obtained yellow solution was freeze-dried to obtain the lignin-based carbon dots, marked as L-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pH/temperature dual responsive hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of PVA and 30 wt% DMSO was prepared and heated to 120 °C for 30 min. 1.53 g N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM), 48 mg n,n′- methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) and 23.97 g water were prepared into a uniform solution, and then 4.5 g of PVA mixture with specified concentration was added and ultrasonic vibration was carried out. The 0.5 mL carbon quantum dots solution, 450 μL N, N, N′, N′- four methyl ethylenediamine (TEMED) and 375 μL 10 wt% ammonium persulfate (APS) were in turn added to the above mixed solution in an ice water swimming. The uniform solution was obtained after mixing. After refrigerating for 24 h, it was restored to room temperature, and the multifunctional hydrogels with dual temperature response could be obtained.
Quantum yield (QY) was determined using a C11347–11 Quantaurus-QY absolute quantum yield measuring instrument (Hamamatsu Photonics Co., Ltd., Japan) equipped with an integrating sphere.

FT-IR spectra of each sample was recorded on a Vector 333 FT-IR spectrometer (Brook Co., Ltd., German). In brief, dried sample was embedded in KBr pellet at a concentration of about 1 mg sample/100 mg KBr. The test resolution was 4 cm−1, and the number of scans was 32.
Raman spectra was determined on a Raman spectrometer (inVia Reflex, Renishaw Co. Ltd., UK).
The UV spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer in the range of 200–600 nm.

The surface composition of each sample was analyzed using a K-ALPHA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd., UK) equipped with an Al Kα (1486.8 eV) as the radiation source.
Clean mica sheet with sample solution was dried at 65 °C. The sample on the mica sheet was examined with a commercial atomic force microscope (XE-100, Park Systems, Korea).
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were taken on a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL Co., Ltd., Japan).

The PL measurement was performed on a F4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Ltd., Japan) equipped with a 150 W Xe lamp under the conditions of slit width 1 nm for excitation and slit width 10 nm for emission at PMT voltage 700 V. Each sample was excited at various wavelengths from 300 to 440 nm with a 20 nm increment. All the optical spectra were collected with a quartz cuvette.

PL decay curves were recorded at room temperature under atmospheric condition using the time-correlated double-photon counting technique for sample transitions with excitation at 320 nm. Two-photon emission spectra were obtained on a FLS920 transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Inc., UK).

The freeze-dried L-CDs were added to 60 mL deionized (DI) water to form L-CDs solution. The solutions with different pH values were prepared from HCl, NaOH, and DI water. After mixing 0.25 mL the L-CDs solution and 9.75 mL a solution at a specific pH value for 5 min, PL spectrum was collected on a F4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Ltd., Japan) equipped with a 150 W Xe lamp.

Freeze-dried hydrogel samples were evenly adhered to the conductive adhesive on the electron microscope. After the surface was sprayed with gold, the micromorphology and pore size of the hydrogel samples were observed by Su-8220 scanning electron microscope (Hitachi Co., Ltd., Japan).

The hydrogel samples with swelling equilibrium at room temperature were placed in HAAKE Mars III rheometer (Thermo Fisher Co., Ltd., Germany) and scanned by frequency. The range of elasticity modulus G′, viscosity modulus G″ and frequency of the hydrogel samples could be obtained from 15.92 Hz to 0.2607 Hz.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of L-CDs are shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b). From the TEM image, it was clearly observed that the particle size of L-CDs was about 2–5 nm. L-CDs were uniformly dispersed in water in the form of monodisperse nanoparticles. The HRTEM image showed more detailed structures of L-CDs. L-CDs had a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, suggesting that they had aromatic rings or graphene-like crystalline structure [43].

AFM image of L-CDs is shown in Fig. 1(c). From the AFM image, it could be seen that the height of L-CDs was about 1–2 nm. Combined with the observation from TEM image, L-CDs apparently existed in the form of spherical-like nanoparticles with an average diameter of 2–5 nm. With the relatively regular shape and the nano diameter, L-CDs is expected to have many promising applications, such as carrier for catalysts or hydrogen storage [44,45].

As shown in Fig. 1(d), the functional groups of L-CDs were examined using a FT-IR spectrometer. In FT-IR spectra, the absorption peaks at 837 cm−1 and 1271 cm−1 were the stretching vibrations of C and N on the aromatic ring. The strong absorption peak at 1394 cm−1 was the bending vibration absorption peak of CN. These peaks indicated that nitrogen was doped onto L-CDs successfully [38]. The absorption peak at 908 cm−1 was the bending vibration of C and H on the benzene ring. The stretching vibration of the syringyl units was at 1328 cm−1, and the CC stretching vibration peak at the aromatic ring was at 1500 cm−1. These absorption peaks clearly indicated that L-CDs retained the aromatic ring characteristics of lignin. The strong absorption peaks at 1581 cm−1 was attributed to CO. This observation suggested that L-CDs had carboxyl groups, which was consistent with the XPS characterization results described later. The vibration absorption peak of 3350–3650 cm−1 was the tensile vibration of OH and NH [46].

In addition, the Raman spectra of the L-CDs was recorded to further confirm the crystalline structure, as shown in Fig. 1(e). It was obvious that there were two Raman peaks at 1352 cm-1 (D) and 1599 cm-1 (G). ID/IG was an indicator of the defect density of crystalline structure, which was 0.91 in the L-CDs. It showed that that there were some graphitized fragments, but some sp2 hybrid functional groups were retained. In order to further determine the elemental composition and chemical bond configuration of L-CDs, XPS study was conducted. From the wide-scan XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 1 (f), it can be seen that there were peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.5 eV, and 530.9 eV, which belonged to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The corresponding elemental composition was C (67.01%), N (15.83%), and O (17.16%), indicating that L-CDs were mainly composed of C, N, and O elements. In addition, the result confirmed that nitrogen was successfully doped onto L-CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectrum reflected the chemical bond configurations of C, N, and O in L-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1 (g)–(i). Deconvolution of the C 1s spectrum displayed four peaks at 284.1 eV, 285.5 eV, 286.9 eV and 287.4 eV, which belonged to CC/CC (61.51%), CN (17.25%), CO (17.03%), and CO/CN (4.21%), respectively [47]. Deconvolution of the N 1s spectrum exhibited two peaks at 399.0 eV and 400.2 eV, which belonged to CN (86.68%) and NH (13.32%), respectively. The N1s spectra clearly shows the formation of pyridine-like N in the L-CDs and successful incorporation of nitro-gen into L-CDs mainly existed in the form of CN [47,48]. Deconvolution of the O 1s spectrum showed three peaks at 530.3 eV, 531.6 eV, and 532.8 eV, which belonged to CO (71.81%), C-OH/Ar-OH (22.85%), and COC (5.34%), respectively. These observations once more confirmed that L-CDs had aromatic structure.

An UV–vis spectrum of L-CDs is shown in Fig. 2(a). The shoulder at 244.5 nm was attributed to the π → π* electronic transition of the conjugated unsaturated structure (CCCO) or the CC bond on the aromatic ring [49]. Compared with the UV–vis absorption peak of pure lignin at 210 nm, the L-CDs showed a red shift. The reason was that the introduction of nitrogen atoms caused the electronic energy levels of L-CDs to change [37]. There was a shoulder at 280 nm, which was caused by the n → π* electron transition of the CO bond in L-CDs [49]. The shoulder of L-CDs at 280 nm blue-shifted, compared with that of lignin, which had an adsorption at around 290 nm. The blue shift was probably attributed to the aromatic ring substituents of L-CDs, which increased the steric hindrance. As a result, the degree of conjugation was reduced and the n → π* electron transition became more difficult. The absorption wavelength became shorter eventually [42]. There was also a broad absorption peak at 350 nm, belonging to the n → π* transition between the O/N-containing group and the sp2 domains [50] (surface energy traps were caused by doping with N atoms) [51–53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence spectra (PL) and luminescence decay time
ce:section.ce:para: The L-CDs emitting blue light were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. The quantum yield of L-CDs was 43.9%, much higher than those under other conditions. Different excitation wavelengths were selected to study the photoluminescence (PL) emission of L-CDs, as shown in Fig. 2 (b)–(c). Slit width of 1 nm was used for excitation and 10 nm for emission at PMT voltage 700 V. L-CDs were excited by varying wavelengths from 300 to 440 nm with a 20 nm increment. It seems that the photoluminescence range was independent of the excitation wavelength between 300 and 400 nm and fell between 385 nm and 615 nm. However, the intensity of the photoluminescence was affected by the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 nm to 380 nm, the photoluminescence peaks remained at about 452 nm, and the intensity increased with the excitation wavelength and reached the maximum at 380 nm. However, when the excitation wavelength was further increased from 380 nm to 440 nm, not only the photoluminescence peaks red-shifted from 452 nm to 504 nm gradually, but also the photoluminescence intensities declined significantly. Similar to that reported in previous studies [54,55], L-CDs also had an excitation dependent behavior, which was probably related to the oxygen- and/or nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of L-CDs [50]. In L-CDs, a large number of surface defects (the oxygen- and/or nitrogen-containing functional groups) were carried over by EDA, and thus the surface energy traps were formed, causing the excitation dependent behavior.

When the carbon dots were excited instantaneously, the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum in a short time and then decayed exponentially. The fluorescence decay curve of L-CDs is shown in Fig. 2 (d). The result could be fitted into a nonlinear first-order exponential model (Eq. (1)):
(1)
R

t τ
where A is the fractional intensity, and τ is the fluorescence lifetime. After fitting, the fluorescence lifetime of L-CDs was 10.89 ns. The correlation coefficient R2 was 0.9989, indicating that the results fitted the model well. The single exponential behavior of the fluorescence lifetime indicated that there was only one emission site for the L-CDs, so the fluorescence state was caused by the surface traps [56].

The fluorescence lifetimes of L-CDs prepared under different conditions are shown in Fig. 3 (a)–(c), which were all longer than 10 ns. With the increase of lignin concentration, hydrothermal temperature and hydrothermal time, the fluorescence lifetimes of L-CDs were extended in general. With lignin concentration 0.125% at 180 °C for 9 h, a maximal fluorescence lifetime (12.20 ns) was obtained.

When the L-CDs absorbed enough photons, the valence band electrons were excited and left corresponding holes in the valence band. When the excited electrons transited back to a lower energy level, they recombined with the holes and emitted fluorescence. According to the published literature [37,38,57], most of the carbon dots prepared from lignin had a fluorescence lifetime of about 5 ns. However, the fluorescence lifetime of the L-CDs prepared in the present work was longer than 10 ns, suggesting that they might be suitable for the applications in photocatalysis [58], photoelectric field [59], biosensing, bacteriostasis, drug/gene carrier [60–62] and other biomedical fields. The significantly increased fluorescence lifetime indicated that the surface defects caused by doping with nitrogen had separated photoelectrons and holes effectively [63].

The images of L-CDs in aqueous solution with different pH values under (d) sunlight and (e) UV light at 365 nm are shown in Fig. 3. It is clear that the UV induced fluorescence at the pH below 3 or above 12 was much weaker than that at pH 3–12.

Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of L-CDs excited by UV at 365 nm within the pH range from 1 to 13.5 are shown in Fig. 3 (f) and (g). The photoluminescence intensity increased gradually with pH from 1 to 10. Meanwhile, the emission peaks blue-shifted from 481 nm to about 452 nm and then stayed at 452 nm. Within this pH range, the fluorescence intensity of L-CDs increased linearly with pH value, following the relationship y = 1022x − 672 (y is the fluorescence intensity of the L-CDs solution, and x is the pH value of the L-CDs solution) with a correlation coefficient R2 0.9867. We can not only adjust the fluorescence emission of L-CDs by adjusting the pH of the aqueous solution easily, but also use L-CDs to measure pH as fluorescent nanoprobes [64,65]. The change of pH may cause electronic transition changes of π-π* and n-π* in graphite nanodomains by refilling or depleting their valence bands [66]. By combining with OH−, the corresponding surface-modified organic shell layers (protective shells on the surface) of L-CDs are formed. To a certain extent, L-CDs are isolated to reduce the rate of non-radiative recombination. And L-CDs show enhanced fluorescence eventually [67]. At pH 10, the photoluminescence intensity of L-CDs reached the maximum value. Very interestingly, pH 10 was the turning point of the photoluminescence intensity. Beyond pH 10, the photoluminescence intensity decreased sharply and linearly within pH 10 and 13. Meanwhile, the emission peaks gradually red-shifted from 452 nm to 459 nm, which could probably be because the protonation and deprotonation of functional groups (such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups) on the surface of the L-CDs led to the Fermi energy level shifting [68]. The pH-responsibility of L-CDs also indicates that surface defects may act as capturing centers for energy carriers, causing corresponding fluorescence changes [69]. The results and observations above indicated that L-CDs had the property of pH-responsibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology and structure of pH/dual responsive hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The multifunctional hydrogels with dual pH/temperature response were fabricated. To compare the effect of different structures on performances of the hydrogels, hydrogels with the same PVA solution but different wt% values (0, 6, 8, 10 and 12) were fabricated and called PNIPAM, IPN1, IPN2, IPN3 and IPN4, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 (a)–(j), the SEM images indicate PNIPAM hydrogels and IPN composite hydrogels exhibit typical porous honeycomb structures. PNIPAM hydrogels have thicker pore walls with the largest diameter. The cross-linked density of the hydrogels increased and formed a structure of two networks as the amount of PVA increased. The internal hydrogen bonding of hydrogels also made the three-dimensional network structure more intensive, which leads to the pore wall of the IPN composite hydrogels gradually thinner and the pore diameter gradually smaller. Meanwhile, the uniformity of the three-dimensional network structure of the IPN hydrogels also gradually improved with the increase of the amount of PVA, which meaned that the cross-linked density could be controlled by changing the amount of PVA and the pore size inside the IPN hydrogel could be adjusted.

The FT-IR spectra of dried PNIPAM hydrogels and IPN composite hydrogels showed the characteristic absorption peak of pH/temperature dual responsive hydrogels. The PNIPAM hydrogels and IPN composite hydrogels exhibited similar infrared spectra, indicating that the functional groups of pH/temperature double responsive hydrogels would not change significantly with the increase of PVA (Fig. 4(k)). In addition, Fig. 4(i) demonstrates that the stretching vibration peak of -OH changes regularly with the increase of PVA. The -OH stretching vibration peaks near 3282 cm−1 and 3396 cm−1 is red shifted to the minimum at 8 wt% PVA (i.e. 3275 cm−1 and 3388 cm−1, respectively), which shows that the hydrogen bonding effect of IPN composite hydrogels is the weakest when 8% PVA is usEd.)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence property of pH/temperature dual responsive hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of IPN composite hydrogels were studied by using different excitation wavelengths. The corresponding spectra was shown in Fig. 4(m). The excitation wavelength ranged from 300 nm to 380 nm with an increment of 20 nm. Under the excitation of 300 nm to 360 nm, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased. The fluorescence intensity reached the maximum when excited at 360 nm and the fluorescence intensity decreased under the excitation from 360 nm to 380 nm. Under the excitation of different wavelength, the emission peak of hydrogels was almost at 456 nm, which was consistent with the that of L-CDs. This meaned that L-CDs was mainly connected with PNIPAM and PVA polymer molecules through its surface groups, instead of connection between the carbon core of L-CDs and the PNIPAM and PVA polymer molecules. Therefore, the number and type of L-CDs surface defects (surface oxygen and/or nitrogen functional groups) remained basically unchanged in the IPN composite hydrogels and the surface energy traps did not change significantly, which gave rise to the same fluorescence properties of IPN composite hydrogels and L-CDs.

The picture of IPN composite hydrogels soaked in different pH conditions under natural light and 365 nm ultraviolet lamp was shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b). By adding HCl or NaOH to adjust the pH of the solution, the fluorescence intensity of the IPN composite hydrogels under UV excitation was greatly reduced when pH < 3 or pH > 11. Fig. 5 (c) and (d) showed the photoluminescence (PL) spectra and PL intensity curves of IPN composite hydrogels soaked in different pH conditions at 360 nm. The fluorescence intensity of IPN composite hydrogels increased gradually from pH 1 to pH 7, and the emission peak gradually shifted from 470 nm to 454 nm, and then basically stabilized at 454 nm. When pH = 7, the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum. Within this pH range, the fluorescence intensity of IPN composite hydrogel showed a linear relationship with the change of pH value. The linear equation is y = 57.445× + 57.745 (y is the fluorescence intensity of IPN composite hydrogel, x is pH value of IPN composite hydrogel), and the coefficient of determination is R2 = 0.9674. Therefore, it can be used as a fluorescent probe for pH measurement. When 9 ≤ pH ≤ 13, the fluorescence intensity decreases rapidly and the emission peak is basically stable at 454 nm. This may be due to the fact that the surface groups of L-CDs are connected with PNIPAM and PVA molecules, and the relative contents of amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of L-CDs are different from those of pure L-CDs, which leads to the difference of Fermi energy level shift [68], which finally shows the difference of pH responsiveness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorbent property of pH/temperature dual responsive hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: In practical applications, absorbent properties hold the key to hydrogels. Fig. 5(e) reveals the relationship between the equilibrium swelling ratio of PNIPAM hydrogels and IPN composite hydrogels at the temperature range of 27 to 44 °C. At lower temperature (27 °C), the equilibrium swelling ratio of hydrogels gradually goes up with the raise of PVA. When PVA reached 8 wt% or more, the equilibrium swelling ratio of IPN composite hydrogels hardly changes with PVA used, because PVA contains more hydroxyl groups and has strong affinity with water. Therefore, the equilibrium swelling ratio of hydrogels ascends with additional PVA. (at almost all temperatures.) When PVA content reaches 8 wt% or more, however, there are some hydrogen bonds formed between internal hydroxyls, limiting its further swelling. The transformation temperature of PNIPAM and IPN composite hydrogels is about 33 °C. The transformation temperature, independent of other kinds and contents of other components, is controlled by the PNIPAM component with temperature response. As shown in Fig. 5(e), the transformation temperature is approximately 32 °C. Over this temperature, the hydrophilic-hydrophobic balance of the PNIPAM in hydrogels is destroyed and the hydrogen bonds formed gradually decrease, and the internal structure begins to collapse and shrink, which leads to a sharp decrease in the equilibrium swelling rate of hydrogels. However, with the PVA content is greater than 10 wt%, the inflection point of transformation temperature becomes less and more obvious because the hydroxyl groups of PVA form hydrogen bonds with each other, and the attraction to water is further enhanced, which reduces the sensitivity to temperature.

The de-swelling kinetic curve can not only reflect the response speed of the hydrogel to temperature, but also reveal the internal network structure of the hydrogel and the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of the polymer chain from the side, and it is shown in Fig. 5(f).

The volume of swelling equilibrium hydrogels shrinks immediately with hydrogels is immersed in deionized water at 37 °C. When at the fifth minute, the water retention ratio of PNIPAM and IPN1, IPN 2, IPN 3 and IPN 4 is 39.2%, 61.9%, 84.5%, 77.0% and 75.1%, respectively. PNIPAM has the fastest response to temperature. The temperature response rate of hydrogels is decreased with the increase of PVA content. The temperature response speed of hydrogels is the slowest at the 8 wt% content of PVA and begins to accelerate after that. Within the range of 5–60 min, the order of swelling of hydrogels is consistent with the order of the first 5 min. When the temperature of deionized water is higher than that of PNIPAM, the retention ratio of hydrogels declines sharply as the hydrophilic/hydrophobic equilibrium state of the surface is destroyed, and its internal structure starts to collapse and contract. When the content of PVA is less than 8 wt%, the temperature sensitivity of hydrogels decreases, because PVA is not enough to form drainage channels, which can result in the increase of water retention ratio of IPN hydrogels with additional PVA. When the content of PVA is larger than 10 wt%, the water retention ratio decreases, because the drainage effect of IPN hydrogel PVA segment is greater than that of PVA hydrophilic. Once the structural collapse occurs, the water molecules are more easily squeezed out of the hydrogels. The temperature response of PVA, thus, can be adjusted by controlling the dosage of PVA, thereby can be applied to the drug delivery system.

PNIPAM hydrogels and IPN composite hydrogels are obtained by freeze drying first and then the reswelling kinetics are studied at 25 °C of deionized water. The results are shown in Fig. 5(g). PNIPAM hydrogels and IPN composite hydrogels exhibit a high reswelling rate in the first 8 h. The swelling ratios of PNIPAM, IPN1, IPN 2, IPN 3 and IPN 4 are 13.28, 10.60, 6.08, 4.20 and 3.16, respectively. PNIPAM and ipn1 have almost reached the swelling equilibrium (the equilibrium swelling ratios of PNIPAM and IPN1 are 13.60 and 11.52 respectively). With the increase of PVA content, the crosslinking density of the internal network structure of hydrogels gradually increases, and the pore size decreases instead. The internal network of IPN hydrogels is difficult in expanding and the reswelling rate of IPN hydrogels decreases due to the poor swelling ability of PVA. The reswelling rate declines after 8 h. The reswelling ratio of IPN composite hydrogels slows down when the content of PVA increases. PVA can get strong attractive to water with numerous hydrophilic functional groups, leading to the extending of the swelling equilibrium time of IPN hydrogels with the increase of PVA content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rheological kinetics of pH/temperature dual responsive hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical strength of hydrogels can be characterized by rheological tests to some extent. The relationship between the elastic modulus G′, the viscosity modulus G″ and the measured frequency of PNIPAM hydrogels and IPN composite hydrogels was shown in Fig.5(h) and Fig.5(i). IPN composite hydrogels with pH/temperature response and good mechanical properties can be prepared by PNIPAM and PVA hydrogels through simple combination. When the frequency decreased from 15.92 Hz to 0.2607 Hz, the elastic modulus G′ of PNIPAM hydrogels and IPN composite hydrogels kept unchanged, but the viscosity modulus G″ decreased. The measured value of the elastic modulus G′ of hydrogels is much larger than that of the viscous modulus G″, which indicates that hydrogels prepared in the experiments exhibits typical viscoelastic solid characteristics and excellent gel properties. As shown in Fig. 5(h), G′ reaches its peak from 580 Pa to 4300 Pa and then decreases to 2300 Pa as the increase of PVA content, while G″ remains constant. This indicates that the content of PVA plays an important role in controlling the mechanical strength of hydrogels. During the G′ rising, the cross-linked density between PVA and PNIPAM increases and the network structure of hydrogels become more increasingly compact with additional PVA, leading to the improvement of the mechanical strength of hydrogels. The cross-linked density of PVA and PNIPAM is the highest at IPN2. Increasing the amount of PVA would cause PVA self-crosslinking of linear polymers, which is not beneficial for maintaining the shape of hydrogels and result in the decrease of G′ [70]. The hydrogels formed hydrogen bonds after containing PVA and L-CDs, and its fracture energy dissipates energy to a certain extent. Therefore, compared with PNIPAM hydrogel, IPN hydrogels can exhibit higher mechanical strength. In addition, L-CDs were linked by hydrogen bonds to PVA and PNIPAM in hydrogels, so it was impossible for hydrogels to lose fluorescence and pH responsiveness because L-CDs failed to dissolve in solution too much.

In this study, lignin-based carbon dots (L-CDs) were successfully synthesized from low-cost and renewable lignin as a carbon source using a facile method. The L-CDs showed graphene-like crystalline structure and were spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of 2–5 nm. They were well dispersible in water and had an excitation dependent behavior. The fluorescence lifetimes of the L-CDs were longer than 10 ns, which were much higher than those reported in the literature. Under the optimal synthesis conditions, the quantum yield was 43.9%. In the range of pH 1–10, the fluorescence intensity and pH value had a linear relationship, suggesting that L-CDs could be used as fluorescent nanoprobes for measuring pH. The L-CDs have potential applications in the fields of biosensing and environmental monitoring. In addition, the pH/temperature double responsive hydrogels were obtained by simple and quick free radical polymerization. The measured value of the elastic modulus G′ of the hydrogels was much higher than that of the viscous modulus G″, and had the typical characteristics of viscoelastic solid. The addition of PVA could form hydrogen bonds with L-CDs, and the fracture energy could dissipate energy. Therefore, the mechanical strength of IPN composite hydrogels was stronger than that of PNIPAM hydrogels. The skeleton of hydrogels had a typical porous honeycomb structure, which could control the internal pore size by adjusting the content of PVA. Within the range of pH 1–7, the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogels has linear relationship with the pH value. Combined with the temperature responsiveness, it is expected to be applied in tissue engineering scaffolds, drug carriers and other fields.
